question: For the `Point` class (from Q10 or similar with `x`, `y` members), overload the `==` operator to compare two `Point` objects. Two points are considered equal if their `x` and `y` coordinates are the same.

test_cases.input: Point p1(1, 1); Point p2(1, 1); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(1, 2); Point p2(1, 1); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(2, 1); Point p2(1, 1); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(0, 0); Point p2(0, 0); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(-5, -10); Point p2(10, 20); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(-5, -10); Point p2(-5, -10); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;
Point p1(10, 20); Point p2(-5, -10); std::cout << bool_to_string(p1 == p2) << std::endl;

errors: The `operator==` contains a logical flaw where it incorrectly returns `true` for any comparison if the `this` point's x-coordinate is negative, regardless of the other point's coordinates. This leads to incorrect comparisons for a subset of valid inputs.
